zone.soilCharacter: sandy, slightly acidic (pH 5.5-6.5), excellent drainage, low organic matter without amendment
zone.summerExtremes: humid, 90-95°F daytime, afternoon thunderstorms common Jun-Sep
zone.winterExtremes: mild, occasional frost Dec-Feb, hard freezes rare
zone.monarchFlywayWindow: Sep 25 - Nov 5 (peak migration through Camden / Cumberland Island)
zone.darkSkyAccess: Cumberland Island National Seashore — Bortle 3 within 20 miles
zone.note: Zone is generous. Full sun is the operator's main constraint, not cold. Most plants below tolerate -8°C / 18°F minimums; satsuma and fig are the cold-edge cases.

audience.primary: S.J., W.J. — operator children (initials only, per .cursorrules)
audience.agePoseStanding: knee-high to waist-high planting elevations preferred so children can plant, water, and harvest unaided

designPrinciples.name: enclosure
designPrinciples.rule: Children need a place to enter, not just walk past. Sunflower fortress, willow tunnel, or bean teepee is non-negotiable.

designPrinciples.name: tasteable-by-month
designPrinciples.rule: Something ripe to taste in every month of the year. Stagger plantings so the garden is always returning a small reward.

designPrinciples.name: kid-sized-tools
designPrinciples.rule: A 'do' garden, not a 'watch' garden. Digging zone with kid-scale shovels and gloves. No off-limits beds in the kid-territory zones.

designPrinciples.name: sit-and-look
designPrinciples.rule: A bench, stump, or stone for sustained observation. Bumblebee-watching, monarch-watching, beetle-watching all require pausing.

designPrinciples.name: sensory-zoning
designPrinciples.rule: Each sensory channel (smell, touch, taste, sound, sight) gets its own intentional zone — AuDHD-friendly opt-in/out. No overstimulating mix-and-mash.

designPrinciples.name: ownership-square
designPrinciples.rule: Each child gets a marked plot they fully own. Inside the square: their decisions. Outside: family-shared. Sovereignty practice.

designPrinciples.name: yes-zone
designPrinciples.rule: One bed where children can pick anything anytime, no permission. Cut-and-come-again flowers + soft herbs. Teaches that other zones have rules without making rules feel like rejection.

designPrinciples.name: calendar-ritual
designPrinciples.rule: Plant one new thing together every solstice and equinox. Garden becomes the year. Four times = four memories per year.

plants.pollinationNote: plant at least two cultivars within 50 ft for cross-pollination
plants.reasonHere: Camden's sandy acidic Coastal Plain soil is rabbiteye paradise. Three bushes outproduce a household — every July you have jam ingredients.

plants.kidHeightAtMaturity: fruit reachable from ground; tree is multi-stem 8-15 ft
plants.reasonHere: One tree outproduces a family. Fruit ripens to soft sweetness right off the limb. Kids will eat them faster than you can pick them.
Cold-hardy citrus on the zone-9a edge. Fragrant in spring bloom. Easy-peel kid-friendly fruit in winter. Doubles as kid's named tree.
Native — feeds monarchs without the disease pressure of tropical milkweed. Camden is on the fall migration corridor. Caterpillar → chrysalis → butterfly visible Sep-Nov in your own yard.
plants.doNotSubstituteWith: Asclepias curassavica (tropical milkweed) — disrupts monarch migration timing, accumulates OE pathogen

plants.reasonHere: Pairs with swamp milkweed for the dry edges of the pollinator flyway. Bright orange flat-topped flowers — monarchs land like a runway.
Native, indestructible. Crush a leaf — smells like fruit punch. Maroon spring flowers smell like pineapple-banana-strawberry. A smell-zone anchor.
Native. Drips with white spring blooms — looks like snow that didn't reach the ground. Excellent candidate for a child's named tree (slow-grow, hugged-trunk-friendly).
First color of spring — bare branches break out in magenta-pink. Native. Good named-tree candidate; flowers are edible (slightly sweet, kids love picking).
Build-material for the sunflower fortress. By August it's a 9-foot ring of stalks with a child-sized doorway gap. Pole beans climb the stalks (Three Sisters style).

plants.kidHeightAtMaturity: vines up to 8-10 ft on support
plants.reasonHere: Climbs sunflower stalks (Three Sisters companion). Productive enough kids snap-pick all summer. Edible green.
Anchors the moonlight-bed trellis. Watching a flower open in 30 seconds at dusk is the after-dinner ritual. Fragrant.
Moonlight-bed companion. Tall white tubular flowers, fragrant after dusk. Hawkmoth visitors at twilight.
Silver-soft fuzzy leaves, irresistible to pet. Anchors the touch-zone of the moonlight bed. Mirrors lunar light.
Releases herbal smell when stepped on barefoot. One of the six tiles in the sensory barefoot path.

plants.containmentRule: Plant in container or bed bordered with deep edge — runners spread aggressively in zone 8b.
plants.reasonHere: Tasteable any time. Resilient to picking. Pair with the yes-zone or smell zone.
Edible peppery flowers — kids pick and eat directly. Round leaves catch dewdrops kids love. Pair with the yes-zone.
Picked-and-popped reward. The bridge between yes-zone and pizza-circle. Sungold is the operator's likely first pick — kids prefer sweet.
Yes-zone anchor. Cut-and-come-again — picking actually triggers more flowers. Pollinator magnet.
Yes-zone tall layer. Self-seeds — once planted, returns. Lacy foliage moves in wind, contributes to wind-library visual.
Pinch the flower sides — it snaps open like a tiny dragon mouth. Cool-season — flowers when zinnias and cosmos are not yet up.
Pollinator-flyway nectar layer. Bumblebees and hummingbirds visit constantly. Crushed leaf smells like Earl Grey.
Pollinator-flyway anchor. Goldfinches strip the seed heads in fall — bird-watching included free with the bloom.
Wind-library visual + sound. Pink fall flower haze — ethereal. Native. Whispers in wind, predictable sound.

plants.harvestWindow: Apr - Jun + Sep flush in zone 8b
plants.reasonHere: Ground-level kid harvest. Pair with rabbiteye blueberry for staggered ripening. Use straw mulch — runners root and spread.
Native screen / hedge. Red winter berries feed birds (NOT children — operator-only tea from leaves possible later, not in the kid zone). Indestructible structural backbone.
plants.kidSafetyNote: Berries are mildly toxic if eaten in quantity — site this where kids do not graze. Leaves OK (true source of caffeine in North American native flora) but operator-only.

structures.label: Sunflower fortress
structures.shape: circle, 6-foot diameter, one doorway gap (2 ft wide), stalks plant on 12-inch centers
structures.buildSeason: April direct-seed; harvest mid-August through October
structures.kidExperience: Children duck through doorway into a green room you can't quite see into from outside. Inside is shaded, hum of bees, edible walls.
operatorBuildSteps: Mark a 6-foot circle in the chosen full-sun bed. Mark one 2-foot doorway gap.
Direct-seed Mammoth Russian sunflower seeds 12 inches apart along the marked circle (skipping the doorway gap).
Two weeks after sunflower germination, plant 2-3 Kentucky Wonder pole bean seeds at the base of every other sunflower.
Water deeply twice a week for first month, then weekly.

structures.label: Sensory barefoot path
structures.shape: linear path, 6 stepping zones each ~18 inches square

zones.material: cedar wood rounds set flush, sealed end-grain
moss patch (sheet moss in shade pocket)
soft white sand 2 inch deep, contained

structures.kidExperience: Each square is one clear sensation. AuDHD-friendly: predictable, opt-in. Walk the whole path or pick one zone.

structures.label: Moonlight bed
structures.shape: rectangular bed, ~4 ft × 8 ft, with central trellis arch
structures.kidExperience: After-dinner walk destination. Sunset open of moonflowers, fragrance of nicotiana, lamb's-ear petting, hawkmoth visitors. Quiet hour between dinner and bedtime.

structures.label: Pollinator flyway
structures.shape: long border, 3-4 ft wide, ideally running south side
structures.kidExperience: Watch-only zone. Caterpillar → chrysalis → butterfly cycle visible Sep-Nov. Bumblebees, hummingbirds, goldfinches all on the same strip.
structures.criticalNote: ONLY native milkweeds (Asclepias incarnata, A. tuberosa). Tropical milkweed (A. curassavica) disrupts monarch migration timing in southern zones — do not plant.

structures.label: Wind library
structures.shape: vertical cluster — three sound sources at three heights
structures.kidExperience: Three predictable sounds layered by wind speed. AuDHD: predictable = safe. The wind tells them about itself.

structures.label: Yes zone
structures.rule: Children may pick anything in this zone, anytime, with no permission required.
structures.kidExperience: Sovereignty practice in miniature. The zone teaches that other zones have rules without making rules feel like rejection.

structures.label: S.J.'s square
structures.shape: marked plot, 4 × 4 ft, stones or hand-painted sign at corners
structures.rule: Inside the square, S.J. decides. Outside, the family decides together.
structures.kidExperience: First arena of garden agency. Children pick the plants, watering schedule, layout. Failure is a feature — they learn what works by trying.

structures.label: W.J.'s square
structures.shape: marked plot, 4 × 4 ft, stones or hand-painted sign at corners
structures.rule: Inside the square, W.J. decides. Outside, the family decides together.
structures.kidExperience: Same as S.J.'s square. Distinct location so each child has visible territory.

structures.label: Digging zone
structures.shape: open bed or sand pit, 4 × 4 ft minimum, kid-sized tools nearby
structures.rule: No plants required. Dig, fill, dig again. Tools: child-scale shovel, trowel, watering can, gloves.
structures.kidExperience: The 'do' garden. Especially important for younger / dysregulated moments — physical labor is grounding.

structures.label: Grandmother bench
structures.shape: bench or large flat stone, shaded, with a view of the whole garden
structures.rule: A quiet sit-and-look spot. For when one of them needs to leave the action.
structures.kidExperience: Co-regulation anchor. Operator can sit beside without facing — side-by-side reduces sensory load vs. eye contact.

structures.label: Stone circle (outdoor classroom)
structures.shape: 5-7 flat seating stones in a ~6 ft diameter circle, optional fire-pit center
structures.kidExperience: Telescope station for night sky (Cumberland Island darkness is 20 min away). Storytime. S'mores.

seasonalPlantingCalendar.ritual: Plant one new thing together every solstice and equinox — four times a year. The garden becomes the calendar.

fieldMapping.molecules_today: Plants encountered, e.g. ['rabbiteye-blueberry', 'monarch-chrysalis', 'C5H8 isoprene from bee balm']
fieldMapping.current_action: What kid is doing in the physical garden: 'building' (sunflower fortress), 'watching' (pollinator flyway), 'planting' (seasonal), 'tasting' (yes-zone), 'sitting' (grandmother bench)
fieldMapping.current_element: Focus element of the moment: 'carbon' for compost, 'phosphorus' for blueberries (leaf), 'calcium' for eggshells in soil
fieldMapping.sensory_profile: Active sensory zone if kid is in one: 'cloud_soft_some' (lamb's ear), 'thyme_warm', 'sand_warm', 'moss_cool'

phosBridge.operatorFlow: 1. Kid logs garden observation in quantum-family.html → Garden Log page. 2. Page builds garden_state JSON. 3. Operator runs `npm run phos:probe` (or pastes via garden-phos-probe.html) to feed Phos. 4. Phos responds with companion text appropriate to what was observed.
constraints: child_id is per-kid stable id, set by operator (not the children's real names per .cursorrules)
Worker requires PHOS_HMAC_SECRET; static page does not hold the secret
garden_state never includes adult names, court terms, school details (matches phos-safety.ts FORBIDDEN list)

transparency.changePolicy: Edit ground-truth, mirror to public/, run npm run verify:garden-zone-8b. Operator approval required for plant additions/removals — this is the household plan, not random landscaping.

version1Constraints: every plant must have a tier in {A, B, C}
every structure must have a tier in {A, B, C}
milkweed plants must be native (incarnata, tuberosa) — never curassavica
